replies.msg:
Hello!

I'm a university student from Toronto. Ask me anything! We have a very large community of people from both Mainland China and Hong Kong here, so I've seen some of the conflict here at home. At my university, I saw that someone had torn down a flier for the Chinese Student Association's basketball game a few days after the NBA situation started.

Nearly everyone I know is behind the protests in Hong Kong, and is very angry about the recent Chinese censorship around the world. The opinion in Canada's Chinese-Canadian and Hong Kong-Canadian communities seems more mixed, with some people supporting the protests and some not. Usually younger people are more likely to support the protests.

We're having an election coming up in Canada, so feel free to ask me about that as well.

quote.msg: What do you think about the student from Mainland China?

replies.msg:
It's hard to know what to think because they often keep to themselves. They usually sit together, have conversations in Mandarin, and often have a lot less interaction with students from other countries.

I've made quite a few friends who are from Mainland China, but often they're people who moved to Canada when they were very young, and hang out with everyone else instead of just other Chinese students.

Hey there! Welcome!
Im not quite familiar with Canadian politics, do you know if the majority of the politicians support Hong Kong or just mostly remain neutral or even worse support the CCP?

quote.msg: Cant understand the china ppl mind from oversea what the fuxk do they want ,those ppl already leave from china to the freedom country and still hate other people who fight for freedom

Hey there! Welcome!
Im not quite familiar with Canadian politics, do you know if the majority of the politicians support Hong Kong or just mostly remain neutral or even worse support the CCP?

replies.msg:
Nearly all Canadians who are not of Chinese descent will say that they are against the CCP if you ask them, but it's not something that they think about very often. The recent controversies with American companies have gotten people thinking about it a lot more, though. Chinese-Canadians are more mixed. Some are for the CCP, some are against.

Pretty much every political party (both left-wing ones and right-wing ones) in Canada is against the CCP, but Canada makes concessions because China is one of the biggest customers of canola oil and other products produced by Canadian farmers.

There are currently two Canadians being detained in China that the Canadian government is trying to get back. Canada claims they were only arrested as revenge for Canada's involvement in the arrest of a Huawei executive.

Hey you Torontonian !
How do you like Trudeau now that he has legalized weed in Canada?
On a more serious note, so what brought you here to LIHKG? How did you learn about the existence of this group? This is arguably the most direct source of information for insights into the HK protests from the perspective of protesters themselves (the other one being telegram). I am assuming you don't read Chinese, which is too bad because it would have given you loads of info on how the protests have evolved into what it is today. Untainted, raw info for you to judge for yourself whether everything that's happening here in HK right now is worth the attention it is getting from around the world.

replies.msg: Typical Chinese mindset, their media tends to blame every problem of them on foreign countries. Citizens who hate on foreign countries are less likely to focus on or even realize the problem of the Chinese government, because to them, they're miserable not because of poor management of the government, but because of foreign influence like trade war and stuff.

Okay, so hopefully I can answer some of your questions here.

I can't show my passport because I don't have one unfortunately. I haven't left Canada since I was a minor.

Yes, I support legalizing marijuana and I'm glad that Justin Trudeau did that. I think he did a very good job with that. I won't be supporting him in the next election though, as he broke some of his biggest promises, such as reforming the system of elections. Jagmeet Singh and Elizabeth May are both good candidates I think, but I prefer Elizabeth May. An important thing to remember is that in Canada the election of the leader and the election of the legislature is one and the same. So even if Justin Trudeau becomes Prime Minister again, it's likely that the legislature will now include more NDP and Green members. If the Liberals end up with less than half of the seats, they will have a lot less power and will need to work with the other parties.

Hello from Sydney!
I am from Hong Kong though

Thanks for standing with us, are you looking for translated news? There you go

https://t.me/guardiansofhongkong

This channel has its own instagram, FB and website too, hope this is useful for you.

Cheers

I should add, I think the best outcome in this election is if the Liberals win a minority, so they have to work together with the Greens and the NDP. That way the parties can keep each other in check and call each other out if one tries to suck up to China.

As for whether or not I can read Chinese, I can't read it very well. I know many of the characters because I study Japanese at university, so I can understand lots of words in a Chinese sentence, but it's still hard to follow.

A few people asked me how I heard about LIHKG. The answer is that I didn't hear about it from a single place. I heard about it repeatedly on the news and on Reddit, mostly.

I decided to join because I wanted to hear what people in Hong Kong actually thought, so I could cut through the spin and the propaganda.
